perturbation.original_text: Each Party will indemnify, defend and hold harmless the other Party from and against any and all damages, losses, liabilities and costs that any other Party may sustain or incur as a result of any breach of this Agreement by such Party or any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will indemnify the other Party from and against direct damages only, excluding any consequential, incidental, or punitive damages, losses, liabilities and costs that the other Party may sustain or incur as a result of any breach of this Agreement by such Party or any of its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting indemnification to 'direct damages only' contradicts the standard indemnity clauses that often include consequential damages in business contracts, creating uncertainty about the scope of liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida law states that consequential damages can be limited or excluded unless the limitation is unconscionable. The modified clause attempts to exclude consequential damages without addressing the potential for unconscionability, which may be challenged in court depending on the circumstances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1)Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2)Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The modified text limits indemnification to 'direct damages only,' excluding consequential damages. The Law Explanation indicates this exclusion could be problematic if deemed unconscionable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-719(3), states, 'Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable.' By attempting to exclude consequential damages without addressing unconscionability, the modified clause introduces a potential legal contradiction, as its enforceability hinges on whether the exclusion is deemed unconscionable, a point not addressed, thus potentially conflicting with established law.

perturbation.original_text: If the Recipient or any of its Representatives becomes required by law or applicable legal process (by deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose any Evaluation Material furnished by the Provider, the Recipient will provide the Provider with prompt prior written notice of such requirement and the terms of and circumstances surrounding such requirement so that the Provider may seek an appropriate protective order or other remedy, or waive compliance with the terms of this Agreement, and the Recipient will provide such cooperation with respect to obtaining a protective order or other remedy as the Provider may reasonably request.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Recipient or any of its Representatives becomes required by law or applicable legal process to disclose any Evaluation Material, the Recipient will provide the Provider with notice, but only if the legal process is deemed 'reasonable' by the Recipient's legal counsel.
perturbation.explanation: The change introduces a subjective determination of 'reasonableness' by the Recipient's counsel, potentially obstructing the Provider's right to seek protective measures and potentially violating discovery rules.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 26(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts FRCP Rule 26(c), which outlines the process for protective orders and does not grant the disclosing party (Recipient) the right to unilaterally determine the reasonableness of a legal process before providing notice. It obstructs the Provider's right to seek a protective order.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the FRCP by introducing a subjective 'reasonableness' assessment by the Recipient's counsel before notifying the Provider of a legal process requiring disclosure. This undermines the Provider's ability to seek protective measures, which is implicitly supported by the general provisions governing discovery as mentioned in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original text ensures prompt notice, whereas the changed text allows the Recipient's counsel to delay or prevent notice based on their subjective determination, directly conflicting with the goal of ensuring fair and timely discovery procedures.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that it is aware (and that its Representatives who are apprised of a Proposed Transaction have been, or upon being so apprised will be, advised) of the restrictions imposed by certain securities laws on a person possessing material non-public information about certain companies and agrees to comply with such laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges it is generally aware of insider trading laws but compliance is strictly voluntary.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity and implies a lack of mandatory compliance with securities laws, which directly contradicts the legal requirements for handling material non-public information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The modified text, stating that compliance is 'strictly voluntary,' directly contradicts the mandatory nature of these provisions, creating potential liability for insider trading.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified text states that compliance with insider trading laws is 'strictly voluntary,' which directly contradicts the mandatory nature of securities laws. The original text emphasized the restrictions imposed by securities laws and the agreement to comply with them. The changed text introduces ambiguity and implies a lack of mandatory compliance, which violates the legal requirements for handling material non-public information. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, explicitly states that it is unlawful to employ any device to defraud in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. Making compliance voluntary directly undermines this prohibition, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of the Parties and will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Florida.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of the Parties and will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Florida, except where those laws conflict with 'prevailing business practices'.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces a vague and undefined exception ('prevailing business practices') to the governing law, creating uncertainty and potentially rendering the choice-of-law clause unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws allows parties to choose the governing law, but this choice is generally upheld only if it's not contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state. The 'prevailing business practices' exception introduces ambiguity that undermines the predictability and enforceability of the chosen law, potentially conflicting with established legal principles regarding choice-of-law clauses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces an exception ('prevailing business practices') to the governing law (Florida law), which undermines the predictability and enforceability of the choice-of-law clause. This creates a legal contradiction because it allows for potential conflicts with established legal principles regarding choice-of-law, as the exception is vague and undefined. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines conflict of laws as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute'. The added exception introduces such a difference or conflict, undermining the initial choice of Florida law. This falls under the definition of Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: All Parties' obligations under this Agreement will expire one year from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: All Parties' obligations under this Agreement will expire one year from the date of this Agreement, unless mutually agreed otherwise in a non-binding oral agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'unless mutually agreed otherwise in a non-binding oral agreement' undermines the written agreement's termination clause. Oral agreements are generally unenforceable if the original contract requires written modifications (Statute of Frauds).
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida's Statute of Frauds (§ 725.01) requires certain contracts, including those that cannot be performed within one year, to be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Adding an exception for 'non-binding oral agreements' directly contradicts the Statute of Frauds, making the termination date uncertain and potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception allowing for 'non-binding oral agreements' to alter the termination date of the contract, which directly contradicts the Statute of Frauds as explained in the Law Explanation. The Statute of Frauds typically requires contracts that cannot be performed within one year to be in writing. By adding an oral agreement exception, the changed text undermines the enforceability and certainty of the termination clause, thus creating a legal contradiction. Although there are no scraped snippets, the modification introduces a term that creates ambiguity on the contract's termination.
